volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Stichus (Section 3.2.473 - Section 4.1.547)

sections.label: Section 3.2.473
sections.original: Promitte .
sections.translation: Do promise me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.473b
sections.original: Certumst .
sections.translation: It ’ s settled .

sections.label: Section 3.2.473c
sections.original: Sic face inquam .
sections.translation: But do , I say .

sections.label: Section 3.2.473d
sections.original: Certa res .
sections.translation: The thing ’ s agreed on .

sections.label: Section 3.2.474
sections.original: Lubente me hercle facies .
sections.translation: By my troth , you ’ d do it with much pleasure to me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.474b
sections.original: Idem ego istuc scio .
sections.translation: I know that well .

sections.label: Section 3.2.475
sections.original: quando usus veniet , fiet .
sections.translation: When an opportunity shall come , it shall be so .

sections.label: Section 3.2.475b
sections.original: Nunc ergo usus est .
sections.translation: Now , then , is the opportunity .

sections.label: Section 3.2.476
sections.original: Non edepol possum .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I cannot .

sections.label: Section 3.2.476b
sections.original: Quid gravare ? censeas .
sections.translation: Why make difficulties ? Do consider ; I have I know not what luxuries at hand [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.2.477b
sections.original: I modo ,
sections.translation: Do be off , now ; seek for yourself another guest for to-day .

sections.label: Section 3.2.479
sections.original: Quin tu promittis ?
sections.translation: You promise , then ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.479b
sections.original: Non graver , si possiem .
sections.translation: I would make no difficulty if I could .

sections.label: Section 3.2.480
sections.original: Vnum quidem hercle certo promitto tibi :
sections.translation: Really , on my word , one thing , for sure , I promise you , I ’ d entertain you with pleasure , beyond a doubt , if you would promise .

sections.label: Section 3.2.482
sections.original: Valeas .
sections.translation: Adieu !

sections.label: Section 3.2.482b
sections.original: Certumnest ?
sections.translation: Have you resolved ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.482c
sections.original: Certum . cenabo domi .
sections.translation: I have resolved . I shall dine at home .

sections.label: Section 3.2.483
sections.original: Quando quidem tu ad me non vis promittere
sections.translation: ( To EPIGNOMUS .) Since you , yourself , are not willing to promise to come to me ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.484
sections.original: sed — quoniam nil processit hac , ego ivero
sections.translation: Since nothing has been effected this way , I ’ ll therefore approach him by a more open path , and I ’ ll speak plainly out .

sections.label: Section 3.2.486
sections.original: vin ad te ad cenam veniam ?
sections.translation: should you like that I should come to dine with you ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.486b
sections.original: Si possim , velim ;
sections.translation: If it were possible , I should like it ; but here are nine other people [*] coming to dine at my house .

sections.label: Section 3.2.488
sections.original: Hau postulo equidem med in lecto accumbere :
sections.translation: For my part , I don ’ t ask that I should recline on the couch ; you know that I ’ m a man for the lower seats .

sections.label: Section 3.2.490
sections.original: At ei óratores sunt popli , summi viri ;
sections.translation: But these are deputies of a people , tip-top men they come here as public ambassadors from Ambracia [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.2.492
sections.original: Ergo oratores populi , summates viri ,
sections.translation: Let then the deputies of a people , your tip-top men , recline at the tip-top place ; I , the lowest , in the lowest quarter .

sections.label: Section 3.2.494
sections.original: Haud aequomst te inter oratores accipi .
sections.translation: It isn ’ t proper for you to be entertained among deputies .

sections.label: Section 3.2.495
sections.original: Equidem hercle orator sum , sed procedit parum .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , and I — I ’ m a deputy , too [*] , but little it does avail me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.496
sections.original: Cras de reliquiis nos volo . multum vale .—
sections.translation: I intend that to-morrow we shall dine upon the scraps . Sincerely , farewell .

sections.label: Section 3.2.497
sections.original: Perii hercle vero plane , nihil obnoxie .
sections.translation: By my troth , ’ tis clear that I ’ m undone , and by no fault of my own [*] . The number is less than it was before by one Gelasimus . I ’ m resolved , hereafter , never to believe in a weasel ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.500
sections.original: nam incertiorem nullam novi bestiam ;
sections.translation: for I know of no beast more uncertain than her . She who herself is ten times a day shifting her place , from her have I taken my omens in matters of life and death to me ! I ’ m determined to call my friends together , to take counsel how by rule I must starve henceforth .

sections.label: Section 4.1.505
sections.original: Íta me di bene amént measque mihi bene servassint filias ,
sections.translation: So may the Gods favour me , and preserve for me my daughters , it is a pleasure to me , Pamphilus , that I see you both return home to your native land , your business prosperously managed , yourself and your brother .

sections.label: Section 4.1.508
sections.original: Satis abs te accipiam , nisi videam mihi te amicum esse , Antipho ;
sections.translation: I should have heard enough from you , Antipho , did I not see that you are friendly to me ; now , since I ’ ve found that you are my friend , I ’ ll give you credence .

sections.label: Section 4.1.510
sections.original: Vocem ego te ad me ad cenam , frater tuos ni dixisset mihi ,
sections.translation: I would invite you to my house to dinner , had not your brother told me that you were going to dine at his house to-day , when he invited myself to his house to dinner . And it would have been more proper for me to give you an entertainment on your arrival , than to engage myself to him , were it not that I didn ’ t wish to disoblige him . Now I don ’ t wish with words alone to insinuate myself into your favour ;

sections.label: Section 4.1.515
sections.original: cras apud me éritis et tu et ille cum vostris uxoribus .
sections.translation: to-morrow you shall be at my house , both you and he , with your wives .

sections.label: Section 4.1.516
sections.original: At apud mé perendie . nam ille heri me iam vocaverat
sections.translation: Then , the day after , at my house ; for it was yesterday he invited me for to-day . But am I quite reconciled to you , Antipho ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.518
sections.original: Quando ita rem gessistis ut vos velle amicosque addecet ,
sections.translation: Since you have thus thrived in your affairs , as it behoves yourselves and persons friendly disposed to wish , let there be good-will and intercourse between us . Take you care to think of this ;

sections.label: Section 4.1.520
sections.original: ut cuique homini res paratast , périnde amicis utitur :
sections.translation: according as wealth is obtained by each man , so does he experience his friends . If his fortunes are flourishing , so are his friends true ; if his prospects decline , so , too , do his friends decline . Fortune finds friends .

sections.label: Section 4.1.523
sections.original: Iam redeo . nimiast voluptas , ubi diu afueris domo ,
sections.translation: I ’ m now returned . ’ Tis a great delight , if you have been long from home , when you return home again , if no anxieties come in contact with your feelings .

sections.label: Section 4.1.525
sections.original: nam ita me absente familiarem rem uxor curavit meam ,
sections.translation: But , in my absence , so well has my wife taken care of my private affairs , that she has made me free and unembarrassed by anxieties . But , see , here ’ s my brother Pamphilus , walking with his father-in-law .

sections.label: Section 4.1.528
sections.original: Quid agitur , Epignome ?
sections.translation: How fares it , Epignomus ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.528b
sections.original: Quid tu ? quam dudum in portum venis ?
sections.translation: How with you ? How long since you came into harbour ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.529
sections.original: Hau longissume .
sections.translation: Not very long ago .

sections.label: Section 4.1.529b
sections.original: Postilla iam iste est tranquillus tibi ?
sections.translation: And is it since then that he has become on smooth terms with you ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.530
sections.original: Magis quam mare quo ambo estis vecti .
sections.translation: More smooth than the sea , on which you have both been borne .

sections.label: Section 4.1.530b
sections.original: Facis ut alias res soles .
sections.translation: You do as you are wont to do other things . Do we unlade the ship to-day , brother ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.531b
sections.original: Clementer volo .
sections.translation: I would rather go quietly to work . Let ’ s rather lade ourselves with delights in their turn . How soon will the dinner be cooked ? I haven ’ t breakfasted to-day .

sections.label: Section 4.1.533b
sections.original: Abi intro ad me et lava .
sections.translation: Go in-doors to my house and bathe .

sections.label: Section 4.1.534
sections.original: Deos salutatum atque uxorem módo intro devortor domum ;
sections.translation: I ’ ll only step home to my own house , to salute the Gods [*] and my wife .

sections.label: Section 4.1.535
sections.original: haec si ita ut volo conficio , continuo ad te transeo .
sections.translation: If I do that as I wish , I ’ ll forthwith return to your house .

sections.label: Section 4.1.536
sections.original: Apud nos eccillam festinat cum sorore uxor tua .
sections.translation: But your wife is hastening to come here with her sister .

sections.label: Section 4.1.537
sections.original: Optumest , iam istoc morai minus erit . iam ego apud te ero .
sections.translation: ’ Tis very good ; there will then be the less delay on that account . I shall be at your house [*] this instant .

sections.label: Section 4.1.538
sections.original: Prius quam abis , praesente ted huic apologum agere unum volo .
sections.translation: Before you go away , in your presence I wish to relate a single story to him .

sections.label: Section 4.1.539
sections.original: Maxume .
sections.translation: By all means .

sections.label: Section 4.1.539b
sections.original: Fuit olim , quasi ego sum , senex ; ei filiae
sections.translation: There was once an old gentleman , just as I now am ; he had two daughters , such as mine now are ; they were married to two brothers ,

sections.label: Section 4.1.541
sections.original: quasi nunc meae sunt vobis .
sections.translation: just as mine now are to yourselves .

sections.label: Section 4.1.541b
sections.original: Miror quo evasurust apologus .
sections.translation: I wonder how the story is to turn out ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.542
sections.original: Erant minori illi adulescenti fidicina et tibicina ,
sections.translation: One of these young men had , as you ( to PAMPHILUS ) now have , a damsel , a music-girl ; he had brought her from abroad , as you have now done . Now , this old gentleman was a widower , just as I now am .

sections.label: Section 4.1.544b
sections.original: Perge porro . praesens hic quidem est apologus .
sections.translation: Do proceed ; this story is really à propos .

sections.label: Section 4.1.545
sections.original: Deinde senex ille illi dixit , cuius erat tibicina ,
sections.translation: Then said this old gentleman to him to whom the music-girl belonged , just as I now say to you —

sections.label: Section 4.1.546b
sections.original: Ausculto atque animum advorto sedulo .
sections.translation: I ’ m listening [*] , and carefully giving heed .

sections.label: Section 4.1.547
sections.original: “ Ego tibi meam filiam , bene quicum cubitares , dedi :
sections.translation: I gave you my daughter , to be a comfortable bedfellow for you ; now , I think it fair that one should be given me in return by you , to be my bed-fellow .
